facts.fact: The four Coffman conditions (mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait) were formally described by Edward G. Coffman Jr. in 1971; all four must be present simultaneously for deadlock to occur.
A mutex ensures only one thread can execute a critical section at a time and maintains strict ownership—only the thread that locked it can unlock it.
A semaphore uses a counter to manage access to a resource; it allows a specified number of threads to access a resource concurrently, with the counter decreasing on acquire and increasing on release.
Binary semaphore does not enforce strict ownership, while mutex strictly requires the owner thread to unlock it.

asyncio in Python uses a single-threaded event loop that switches between tasks during non-blocking I/O operations, leveraging platform-specific support (epoll on Linux, kqueue on macOS, IOCP on Windows).
The cost of task switching in asyncio is much smaller than thread switching, and asyncio can start significantly more tasks than multithreading can start threads for I/O-bound workloads.
Compare-and-swap (CAS) is an atomic instruction that compares a memory location with a given value and modifies it to a new value only if they match—all as a single atomic operation.

Most multiprocessor architectures have supported CAS in hardware since at least 2013, making it the most popular synchronization primitive for implementing both lock-based and lock-free concurrent data structures.

The ABA problem in CAS-based algorithms occurs when a memory location is changed multiple times between reading and CAS attempt, acquiring a bit pattern matching the old value and causing false positive matches.
There are three levels of lock-free guarantees: wait-free (strongest—operations never fail), lock-free (some operations may fail but progress guaranteed in fixed steps), and obstruction-free.
Preventing circular wait can be achieved by enforcing total resource ordering where each resource has a unique number and processes must request resources in predefined order.

PostgreSQL extends deadlock detection to distributed systems through FDW (Foreign Data Wrapper) and dblink by representing wait-for-graphs including remote transactions to detect deadlocks across database servers.

Classical counter increment race condition: two threads each incrementing a shared integer by 1 without synchronization can result in final value of 1 instead of expected 2, due to read-modify-write being non-atomic.
Time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition: checking if a condition exists and acting on that stale observation, where another thread may change state between check and act.
Google's KCSAN tool discovered over 300 race bugs in kernel code; Razzer (IEEE S&P 2019) found more than 30 race bugs, demonstrating race conditions as a significant attack vector.
Race conditions are difficult to debug because results are nondeterministic and depend on thread timing; symptoms disappear in debug mode or with logging added (Heisenbug phenomenon).
Lock-free queues can achieve round-trip latency below 100 nanoseconds in optimal scenarios (same-core producer/consumer), but latency increases 3×+ when threads run on different cores.

Some MPMC (multi-producer multi-consumer) lock-free queues achieve 10× throughput compared to standard mutex-based queues; throughput drops at least 1.5× when producer and consumer run on different cores.

memory_order_seq_cst (sequential consistency) establishes a single total modification order of all atomic operations, requiring full memory fence CPU instructions on multi-core systems and becoming a performance bottleneck.
Acquire-release semantics allow a release store to synchronize-with an acquire load on the same variable; all memory writes before the store become visible in the acquiring thread.

examples.scenario: PostgreSQL simultaneous UPDATE deadlock: two transactions try to update the same rows in different order. Transaction A locks Row 1 then waits for Row 2 (held by Transaction B), while Transaction B holds Row 2 and waits for Row 1 (held by A). PostgreSQL detects and rolls back one transaction.

Banking ATM withdrawal race condition: Two people with same PIN withdraw $200 simultaneously from account containing $1000. Without synchronization, both may see balance as $1000, both withdraw $200, leaving incorrect balance of $800 instead of $600.

Node.js event loop handling 10,000 concurrent WebSocket connections: single-threaded async model efficiently handles I/O with epoll/kqueue rather than spawning 10,000 threads, reducing context switch overhead.
High-frequency trading: Lock-free queue with CAS loop processes 1 million orders/second with sub-100ns latency when producer/consumer share CPU core, enabling real-time trade execution.

Google KCSAN discovering race conditions in Linux kernel: tool found 300+ data races in kernel code, exposing critical bugs where concurrent access to shared kernel structures could cause corruption or privilege escalation.

Citus Data distributed PostgreSQL: coordinating locks across multiple worker nodes requires predicate locks on filter conditions (UPDATE .. WHERE key='x') to prevent distributed deadlocks when same rows modified concurrently.

analogies.analogy: Mutex is like a single-occupancy bathroom: only one person (thread) can lock the door and enter at a time. Once inside, they hold the key until they leave. Others must wait.

Semaphore is like a parking garage with 10 parking spaces: the counter starts at 10. Each car (thread) that enters decrements the counter; when count reaches 0, new cars wait. As cars leave, counter increments.

Circular wait deadlock is like 4 people sitting around a table, each holding one chopstick and needing their neighbor's chopstick to eat. No one can eat because everyone is waiting for someone else to release.

Race condition (TOCTOU) is like checking your bank balance ($1000), deciding to buy something for $200, but a transaction posts between check and purchase, and now you overdraft—the assumption was stale.
Lock-free CAS loop is like optimistically assuming you can make a change, but checking at commit time if someone else changed the value underneath. If yes, retry the entire operation.

Event loop in async/await is like a single cashier serving 100 customers, switching between them during idle time (waiting for payment). No new cashier hired; just better utilization of the one cashier.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A mutex and binary semaphore are equivalent
common_misconceptions.reality: Mutex enforces strict ownership—only the locking thread can unlock. Binary semaphore (counter=1) has no ownership; any thread can release it. This breaks if an unintended thread releases the semaphore.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Using more threads always speeds up I/O-bound workloads
common_misconceptions.reality: Event loop models (single-threaded async) often outperform multithreading for I/O because task switching cost is much lower than thread context switch overhead. 10,000 async tasks > 10,000 threads.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Race conditions only happen during concurrent execution—adding logging prevents them
common_misconceptions.reality: Race conditions are Heisenbugs; logging changes timing and makes them disappear, but the bug still exists. Only proper synchronization fixes them.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Lock-free algorithms are always faster than mutex-protected critical sections
common_misconceptions.reality: Under high contention, CAS loops retry frequently, wasting CPU cycles. Mutex may perform better with high lock contention because threads sleep instead of spinning.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Breaking one Coffman condition is sufficient to prevent all deadlocks
common_misconceptions.reality: Breaking any ONE of the four conditions prevents deadlock. Common approaches: disable hold-and-wait (request all resources atomically), enable preemption, or enforce resource ordering to prevent circular wait.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Sequential consistency (seq_cst) is always the right memory order for atomics
common_misconceptions.reality: seq_cst requires full memory fences and is expensive. Acquire-release semantics are often sufficient and faster; only use seq_cst when true global ordering is required.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Distributed deadlock detection works the same as single-machine deadlock detection
common_misconceptions.reality: Distributed deadlock detection requires wait-for-graphs that span multiple database servers via FDW/dblink, with higher latency and partial visibility of global lock state.

common_misconceptions.misconception: CAS is immune to race conditions
common_misconceptions.reality: CAS-based algorithms suffer from the ABA problem where a value changes multiple times, appearing unchanged to the CAS instruction, causing false positive matches.
